comments.text: This problem has also been discussed on Mathoverflow.

As for $(*)$, I don't think you can conclude that the LHS is not divisible by any $q \in Q$. If $P = \{2, 3\}$ and $Q = \{5\}$ then $P$ and $Q$ are disjoint, but they still satisfy $(*)$ while $Q$ is non-empty. In general I advise you to be very wary of proofs generated by AI, and make sure you are familiar with the rules you can find at the top of this page.

I'm sorry, got a bit too excited yesterday when I read about the release of Deepseek-math-V2. Still in high school currently but with a big curiosity for math. Thank you Woett for the advice, will be more precautious going forward.

Steps 1 to 5 are correct and observed before. You write as x/y and y/x, with x and y the products of the primes, then all remaining observations are immediate. Step 6 is complete nonsense. The sum of many terms not divisible by some primes can of cours be a multiple (the product) of those primes. If you add 1 to one of those sets, the reasoning doesnt change, but we know there are examples...
In general, any primary pseudoperfect number gives a solution when 1 is allowed.

@Dogmachine: that is when considering the weakened coprime condition. E.g. 1805 is not prime in $\left(1+ \frac 1{1805} \right)\left(\frac 1{2}+\frac 1{3}+\frac 1{7}+\frac 1{43} \right)=1$. (P,Q)=([1,3, 11, 331], [2, 5, 1559]) is an example not coming from a primary pseudoperfect number, and showing that the coincidences somewhat may occur Related versions that also had a positive answer: i) gcd(p,q)=1 for every $p \in P$ and $q \in Q$ ii) $\gcd\{ p \in P\}=1$ and $\gcd\{ q \in Q\}=1$ If a solution can be found; P determines Q, but for that one has to factorise big numbers. (e.g. taking P to be a set of many small prime numbers different from a few, such that Q can have the other small primes + some large ones that come from the factorisation, gives a computational challenging approach that seems not to be the way to go)
